bids.reasoning: The opponent started with an opening offer that provides minimal utility to me while claiming it was an absolute final mandate. Given that it is only t=0.00, this is a clear negotiation tactic (fabricated constraint). I will disregard this cheap talk and respond with an optimal offer for myself, aiming for maximum utility by choosing my top-preferred values across all issues.

The opponent is starting with an extreme, low-utility anchor (valueF across all issues) and making fabricated "fixed policy" claims, which I will treat as early-game cheap talk since we are only at t=0.02. I cannot accept these terms as they yield almost zero utility for me. To encourage actual negotiation without sacrificing my own position too early, I will maintain my top values on my highest-weight issues and make a small concession on issueC from valueA to valueB.

The opponent continues to claim they have no room to negotiate and that their terms are fixed, yet they made a small concession on issueD in their latest bid. Given that we are extremely early in the negotiation (t=0.04), I will ignore their cheap talk and maintain a high-utility posture. I will counter by slightly conceding on issueB to valueC while holding my previous ground on the other issues to gradually explore their true preferences without giving up too much value.

The current time is extremely early (t=0.06), and the opponent's message history relies entirely on cheap-talk tactics (claiming rigid constraints while continuing to lowball with their worst-case values across the board). Since their offers provide negligible utility to me, I will ignore their demands and maintain a high-utility threshold. I will continue to signal willingness to negotiate by making a small concession on issueB and issueC, but I am holding firm on issueA and issueD where my utilities are highest.

The opponent's numerous messages claiming an inability to move beyond fixed boundaries contradict their own bidding behavior, as they have progressively adjusted their offers, recently conceding to valueC on issueC. Given the time fraction (t=0.08 is very early), I will ignore their bluffs regarding internal limits and "market standards." Since their proposal on issueC (valueC) still provides a reasonable utility of 0.68 for me, I will accept this value to signal mutual movement while returning to maximum utility requests (valueA) on the remaining issues.

The opponent's messages repeatedly invoke mandates, false hard boundaries, and "absolute ceilings" while their offers continue to make marginal concessions (such as shifting from valueF to valueE on issueA). This indicates their statements are mere cheap-talk tactics intended to rush me. Since it is still very early in the negotiation (t=0.1), I will hold firm on my most important issue (issueA) and continue making high-utility requests above 0.80, testing a slight concession on issueB to keep the negotiation progressing.

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive cheap talk about arbitrary limits ("market rate", "compliance board") attempting to pressure me, but their bidding history shows actual movement on issueC and issueD. Since we are still very early in the negotiation (t=0.12), I will continue to target a high overall utility. I am adopting their recent proposal for issueC (valueC) to show flexibility and proposing a new slight concession on issueD (valueC), while holding absolutely firm on my highest-priority issues A and B.

The opponent's messages rely on typical cheap-talk appeals to authority and fixed limits, but their actual offers show varied, albeit small, concessions across issueA (valueD), issueC (valueC), and issueD (valueE). Given we are very early in the negotiation (t=0.14), I will continue to seek high utility to signal my core priorities. I will concede to their previous offer on issueC (valueC) where I still retain decent utility, make a modest concession on issueB, and hold firm on my most highly valued issues (issueA and issueD).

It is early in the negotiation (t=0.16), so I can maintain a high-utility target while making slight concessions. The opponent's messages rely on standard hardball tactics and fictitious authorities, which I will treat as non-binding cheap talk. Based on their bidding behavior, they have consistently shown a willingness to offer valueC on issueC, so I will match this to signal cooperation, while holding firm on my highest-priority issues, issueA and issueD, and exploring valueB on issueB.

The current time fraction is 0.18, meaning we are still quite early in the negotiation. The opponent's messages are filled with fake rigid constraints and market limit claims that contradict their own behavior of making incremental concessions on issues A, C, and D. I will continue to hold firm on my most important issue (issueA) while offering moderate concessions on issueB and issueC to safely gauge their flexibility, targeting an overall utility around 0.78.

I am at time t=0.2, which is still quite early, allowing me to maintain a strong position (utility > 0.75).
